perturbation.original_text: This Sponsorship Agreement (the "Agreement") is entered into effective January 1, 2010 by and between Stallings Capital Group Consultants, Ltd., a Texas limited partnership dba Bob Stallings Racing ("Racing"), and GAINSCO, INC., a Texas corporation (the "Sponsor").
perturbation.changed_text: This Endorsement Contract (the "Agreement") is entered into effective January 1, 2010 by and between Stallings Capital Group Consultants, Ltd., a Texas limited partnership dba Bob Stallings Racing ("Racing"), and GAINSCO, INC., a Texas corporation (the "Advertiser").
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Sponsorship Agreement' to 'Endorsement Contract' and 'Sponsor' to 'Advertiser' creates confusion about the contract's nature. Later sections refer to 'Sponsorship Fee' (Section 3) and 'Benefits' associated with 'sponsorship' (Section 2), creating a contradiction. It's unclear whether those provisions still apply to an "Endorsement Contract" with an "Advertiser."
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Sponsor shall pay to Racing a sponsorship fee in the amount of $750,000.00 for the term of this Agreement, payable in an initial installment payable on or before February 1, 2010 in the amount of $350,000.00 and ten installments of $40,000.00 on or before the first day of each month commencing March 1, 2010 and ending with the installment due on December 1, 2010 (unless this Agreement is sooner terminated pursuant to Section 14 hereof, in which case Sponsor shall have no obligation to make any payments after the date of termination).
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Sponsorship Agreement' to 'Endorsement Contract' and 'Sponsor' to 'Advertiser' directly contradicts the later use of the terms 'sponsorship fee' and 'sponsor' in the contradicted text. This creates a strong in-text contradiction under the definition of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction, making it unclear whether the 'Advertiser' is obligated to pay a 'sponsorship fee'. The terms are not used interchangeably or defined as equivalent, leading to conflicting obligations as per the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: Racing has invited the Sponsor to continue to act as the primary sponsor of the Racing Team for 2010, and the Sponsor desires to act in that capacity. In consideration of the sponsorship fee provided for herein, the parties desire to enter into this Agreement to govern the terms of such sponsorship in 2010.
perturbation.changed_text: Racing has invited the Advertiser to continue to act as the primary benefactor of the Racing Team for 2010, and the Advertiser desires to act in that capacity. In consideration of the advertisement costs provided for herein, the parties desire to enter into this Agreement to govern the terms of such benefaction in 2010.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Sponsor' to 'Advertiser', 'sponsorship fee' to 'advertisement costs' and 'sponsorship' to 'benefaction' introduces ambiguity. Section 7 refers to 'other sponsors for the Racing Team'. It is now unclear whether advertisement costs and benefaction are same as sponsorship fee in section 3
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Sponsor acknowledges that Racing has arranged and may arrange in the future for other sponsors for the Racing Team. Racing agrees that, during the term of this Agreement, (i) Sponsor shall have the right to approve or disapprove any additional sponsor identified by Racing, and (ii) unless another proposed sponsor has agreed to pay a sponsorship fee that exceeds the amount paid by Sponsor, no other sponsor shall receive any benefit of greater value (including either an equivalent or a more prominent use of another sponsor's name, logo or other identifying information) than the Benefits provided to the Sponsor hereunder.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Sponsor' to 'Advertiser' and 'sponsorship fee' to 'advertisement costs' introduces ambiguity. The original text clearly defines the relationship as a sponsorship with a defined fee. The changed text uses 'advertisement costs' and 'benefaction,' which are less specific and could be interpreted differently. The contradicted text refers to 'other sponsors' and their 'sponsorship fee.' Because of the change, it is now unclear if the 'advertisement costs' are equivalent to a 'sponsorship fee,' which directly impacts the sponsor approval rights outlined in the contradicted section. This ambiguity creates a direct conflict regarding the rights and obligations related to sponsorships versus advertisements. This aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The only rights granted to the Sponsor hereunder are the right to receive the Benefits, and Racing hereby retains all other rights with respect to the Racing Team, including but not limited to logos, symbols, names and other marks and intellectual property of the Racing Team, and any proceeds derived by the Racing Team. The Sponsor hereby retains and does not grant any rights to Racing to use any of its logos, symbols, names or other marks or intellectual property, except for use as described in Section 2 hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The only rights granted to the Advertiser hereunder are the right to receive the Benefits, and Racing hereby retains all other rights with respect to the Racing Team, including but not limited to logos, symbols, names and other marks and intellectual property of the Racing Team, and any proceeds derived by the Racing Team. The Advertiser hereby retains and does not grant any rights to Racing to use any of its logos, symbols, names or other marks or intellectual property, except for use as described in Section 2 hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Replaced 'Sponsor' with 'Advertiser' throughout the section. This creates uncertainty about who possesses and controls specific rights. Section 9 outlines conduct expectations for 'Racing members' that could unfavorably reflect upon the 'Sponsor' which now is referred as 'Advertiser' thus, what actions constitute a reflection on the advertiser is unclear and could lead to disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Racing and all Racing members, including but not limited to all drivers, agree to use best efforts to conduct themselves in such a manner so as not to reflect unfavorably upon the Sponsor or its products. The Sponsor shall have the right to terminate this Agreement on written notice to Racing if any driver, the general manager or any other member of Racing (i) fails to conduct himself/herself in accordance with generally accepted standards of morality, (ii) engages in any activity which reflects adversely on the image, reputation or goodwill of the Sponsor or (iii) disparages the products or services of the Sponsor; provided, however, the Sponsor shall not have the right to terminate this Agreement if Racing, within fifteen (15) days after receipt of written notice by the Sponsor terminates the employment of, or otherwise dismisses from the racing team, the driver(s), general manager(s) or other member(s) of Racing engaging in the offensive conduct. Upon termination, the Sponsor shall be entitled to a pro rata refund of monies paid for services not yet performed by Racing based upon the number of races for the applicable racing season. The Sponsor's decision with respect to all matters arising under this Section shall be conclusive.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Sponsor' to 'Advertiser' introduces a contradiction because Section 9 explicitly grants rights and imposes obligations based on actions that reflect unfavorably upon the 'Sponsor'. If 'Sponsor' is replaced by 'Advertiser' in one section but not the other, it's unclear whose reputation is being protected under Section 9 and who can terminate the agreement based on unfavorable actions. This creates ambiguity and potential disputes about the scope and enforceability of the termination clause.
